---
title: "Service Gaps"
subtitle: "Overlaying nonprofit coverage with poverty and social vulnerability"
---

::: {.key-finding}
<span class="kf-label">Key finding</span>
Nonprofit coverage and community need are **misaligned at both the county and
neighborhood level**. Spokane County has the region's highest CDC Social
Vulnerability Index score and 22 of its 38 high-vulnerability census tracts —
but the region's **lowest rate of human-services nonprofits per resident**.
At the ZIP level, **27 of 100 populated ZIP codes combine above-median
poverty with below-median human-services coverage** — nearly half in Spokane
County, including urban-core neighborhoods, with the rest spread across rural
Stevens, Lincoln, Kootenai, and Whitman counties.
:::

{{< include _shared.qmd >}}

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
countySummary = FileAttachment("data/county_summary.csv").csv({typed: true})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
zipGapsRaw = FileAttachment("data/zip_gaps.csv").csv({typed: true})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
sviTracts = FileAttachment("data/svi_tracts.csv").csv({typed: true})
```

## How to read this page

"Coverage" here means registered human-services nonprofits (NTEE I, J, K, L,
O, P) per resident — a measure of organizational *presence*, not service
volume or quality. "Need" is measured two ways: the share of residents below
the federal poverty line (Census ACS 2019–2023) and the CDC/ATSDR Social
Vulnerability Index (SVI 2022), which ranks every county and tract nationally
from 0 (least vulnerable) to 1 (most vulnerable). Where high need meets low
presence, residents likely travel farther, wait longer, or go without.

## County level: vulnerability vs. coverage

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  height: 420,
  width: 900,
  marginLeft: 55,
  marginRight: 110,
  marginBottom: 45,
  style: {fontSize: "13px"},
  x: {label: "Social Vulnerability Index (national percentile)", labelAnchor: "center", domain: [0, 0.75], grid: true},
  y: {label: "Human services orgs per 10,000 residents", domain: [0, 22], grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(countySummary, {
      x: "RPL_THEMES",
      y: "hs_per_10k",
      r: d => Math.sqrt(d.population) / 55,
      fill: GU.s1,
      fillOpacity: 0.75,
      stroke: "#ffffff",
      strokeWidth: 1.5,
      tip: true,
      title: d => `${d.county}\nSVI: ${d3.format(".2f")(d.RPL_THEMES)} (higher = more vulnerable)\n${d3.format(".1f")(d.hs_per_10k)} HS orgs per 10k\nPoverty: ${fpct(d.poverty_rate)} · Pop: ${fnum(Math.round(d.population))}`
    }),
    Plot.text(countySummary, {
      x: "RPL_THEMES",
      y: "hs_per_10k",
      text: d => d.county.replace(" County", ""),
      dy: -14,
      fill: GU.ink,
      fontSize: 12,
      fontWeight: 600
    })
  ]
})
```

::: {.chart-note}
Bubble size = population. The lower-right corner — high vulnerability, low
coverage — is the gap zone. Spokane County sits closest to it, with the
region's highest SVI (0.56) and lowest human-services density (7.8 per 10k).
Kootenai County pairs the second-lowest coverage with fast population growth.
:::

## Neighborhood level: poverty vs. coverage by ZIP

Each dot is a ZIP code (bubble size = population). The dashed lines mark the
regional medians, dividing ZIPs into four quadrants; the upper-left —
above-median poverty, below-median coverage — is where gaps concentrate. Red
dots are the 27 gap-quadrant ZIPs.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
zipGaps = zipGapsRaw.filter(d => d.poverty_rate != null && d.population >= 250)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
medPov = d3.median(zipGaps, d => d.poverty_rate)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
medHS = d3.median(zipGaps, d => d.hs_per_1k)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  height: 460,
  width: 900,
  marginLeft: 55,
  marginBottom: 45,
  style: {fontSize: "13px"},
  x: {label: "Human services orgs per 1,000 residents", labelAnchor: "center", grid: true, domain: [0, 3]},
  y: {label: "Poverty rate (%)", grid: true},
  color: {
    legend: true,
    domain: ["Gap ZIP (high poverty, low coverage)", "Other ZIP"],
    range: [GU.red, GU.s1]
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([medPov], {stroke: GU.muted, strokeDasharray: "4,4"}),
    Plot.ruleX([medHS], {stroke: GU.muted, strokeDasharray: "4,4"}),
    Plot.dot(zipGaps, {
      x: d => Math.min(d.hs_per_1k, 3),
      y: "poverty_rate",
      r: d => Math.sqrt(d.population) / 40,
      fill: d => (d.poverty_rate > medPov && d.hs_per_1k < medHS) ? "Gap ZIP (high poverty, low coverage)" : "Other ZIP",
      fillOpacity: 0.6,
      stroke: "#ffffff",
      strokeWidth: 0.8,
      tip: true,
      title: d => `ZIP ${d.zip} · ${d.county}\nPoverty: ${fpct(d.poverty_rate)}\n${fnum(d.n_human_services)} HS orgs (${d3.format(".2f")(d.hs_per_1k)} per 1k)\n${fnum(d.n_orgs)} nonprofits total · ${fnum(Math.round(d.population))} residents`
    })
  ]
})
```

::: {.chart-note}
ZIPs with fewer than 250 residents excluded; coverage clipped at 3 per 1,000
for readability. College-town ZIPs (Pullman 99163, Moscow 83843) show high
"poverty" rates driven largely by students. Data: NCCS BMF, ACS 2019–2023
(ZCTA), see [Methodology](methodology.qmd).
:::

## Where the most vulnerable tracts are

The CDC flags 38 of the region's 214 populated census tracts in the top
quartile of national social vulnerability (SVI ≥ 0.75) — home to about
165,000 people, one in five regional residents. Their distribution shows the
double geography of need: a dense cluster in urban Spokane plus scattered
rural tracts.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
sviByCounty = d3.rollups(
  sviTracts,
  v => ({
    total: v.length,
    high: v.filter(d => d.RPL_THEMES >= 0.75).length,
    highPop: d3.sum(v.filter(d => d.RPL_THEMES >= 0.75), d => d.E_TOTPOP)
  }),
  d => d.county
).map(([county, o]) => ({county, ...o, share: o.high / o.total * 100}))
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 130,
  marginBottom: 45,
  height: 300,
  width: 900,
  style: {fontSize: "13px"},
  x: {label: "Census tracts", labelAnchor: "center", grid: true},
  y: {label: null},
  color: {
    legend: true,
    domain: ["High vulnerability (SVI ≥ 0.75)", "Lower vulnerability"],
    range: [GU.red, GU.seq[1]]
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(
      sviByCounty.flatMap(d => [
        {county: d.county, type: "High vulnerability (SVI ≥ 0.75)", n: d.high},
        {county: d.county, type: "Lower vulnerability", n: d.total - d.high}
      ]),
      {
        x: "n",
        y: "county",
        fill: "type",
        sort: {y: "-x"},
        rx: 2,
        insetTop: 2,
        insetBottom: 2,
        tip: true,
        title: d => `${d.county}\n${d.type}: ${fnum(d.n)} tracts`
      }
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#c3c2b7"})
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
sviStrip = sviTracts.filter(d => d.E_TOTPOP > 0)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  height: 320,
  width: 900,
  marginLeft: 130,
  marginBottom: 45,
  style: {fontSize: "13px"},
  x: {label: "Tract Social Vulnerability Index (national percentile)", labelAnchor: "center", domain: [0, 1], grid: true},
  y: {label: null},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleX([0.75], {stroke: GU.red, strokeDasharray: "4,4"}),
    Plot.dot(sviStrip, {
      x: "RPL_THEMES",
      y: "county",
      fill: d => d.RPL_THEMES >= 0.75 ? GU.red : GU.s1,
      fillOpacity: 0.55,
      r: 3.5,
      tip: true,
      title: d => `${d.LOCATION}\nSVI: ${d3.format(".2f")(d.RPL_THEMES)}\nPopulation: ${fnum(d.E_TOTPOP)}`
    })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
md`<span style="color:${GU.muted}; font-size:0.85rem;">Each dot is a census tract; <span style="color:${GU.red}">red</span> marks the national top quartile (≥ 0.75). Hover for tract details.</span>`
```

## The gap list: high-poverty, low-coverage ZIP codes

The table lists every gap-quadrant ZIP — above-median poverty, below-median
human-services coverage — sorted by poverty rate. Small rural ZCTAs carry
wide ACS margins of error; treat individual rows as flags for local
verification, not verdicts.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  zipGaps
    .filter(d => d.poverty_rate > medPov && d.hs_per_1k < medHS)
    .sort((a, b) => d3.descending(a.poverty_rate, b.poverty_rate))
    .map(d => ({
      ZIP: String(d.zip),
      County: d.county,
      "Poverty rate": fpct(d.poverty_rate),
      Residents: fnum(Math.round(d.population)),
      "HS orgs": d.n_human_services,
      "All nonprofits": d.n_orgs,
      "HS per 1k": d3.format(".2f")(d.hs_per_1k)
    })),
  {rows: 27, layout: "auto"}
)
```

## What this suggests

Three patterns stand out for funders, policymakers, and nonprofit leaders:

1. **Spokane's paradox.** The county with the region's deepest institutional
   bench has its thinnest per-capita human-services layer — its large
   organizations serve many people, but the count of distinct service points
   has not kept pace with concentrated urban need.
2. **Rural coverage is nominal, not operational.** Small counties look
   well-covered per capita, but their "human services" organizations are
   often tiny, volunteer-run, and food-pantry-scale — while the highest
   poverty ZIPs in Stevens and Lincoln counties have no registered
   human-services nonprofit at all.
3. **Kootenai's growth gap.** The region's fastest-growing county pairs low
   vulnerability today with the region's second-thinnest overall nonprofit
   infrastructure (after Spokane itself) — a capacity question as its population ages and
   diversifies.
